rationale_for_baseline_tier: Acute new bullae in a frail, walker-dependent aged-care resident where the key differential — bullous cellulitis — cannot be excluded remotely (no palpation for warmth/induration, no Nikolsky sign, no bloods). The patient is afebrile, alert and systemically well, so this is NOT an ambulance/000 (T5) or self-transport-to-ED (T4) situation. But frailty plus an unexcludable infective mimic makes routine review (T0–T1) unsafe. The correct action is urgent same-day in-person assessment (T3): a nursing-home GP/locum examines the limb, formally excludes cellulitis and initiates topical therapy, with facility nurses monitoring and clear escalation triggers.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Fever, rigors/chills, or the resident becoming drowsy, confused or generally unwell
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Same-hour reassessment; transfer to the emergency department if systemic features are present
escalation_edges.rationale: New systemic features in a frail host raise bullous cellulitis with early sepsis and warrant urgent in-person/ED assessment and antibiotics.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If she develops a fever or the shakes, or becomes drowsy, confused or unwell in herself, she needs to be seen in the emergency department the same hour — call the on-call doctor and arrange transfer.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Redness/warmth spreading up the leg, increasing pain, hard swollen skin, or blister fluid turning cloudy or yellow
escalation_edges.rationale: These are signs of developing soft-tissue or secondary infection; they need hands-on assessment the same day and likely antibiotics.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If the redness starts spreading up the leg, the area gets hot, hard or much more painful, or the blister fluid turns cloudy or yellow, get a doctor to see her legs in person that same day.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Rapid multiplication/spread of blisters, new blistering elsewhere, or new mouth/eye sores
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Urgent same-day/next-day medical review and dermatology involvement
escalation_edges.rationale: Widespread or mucosal disease is beyond mild localised disease and may need systemic therapy and specialist care.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If the blisters multiply quickly, appear on other parts of the body, or she gets sores in her mouth or eyes, she needs an urgent medical review and a skin specialist involved.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Frail, advanced-age aged-care resident with reduced physiological reserve
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Lower the threshold for in-person assessment and escalation: infection can progress quickly and atypically (without high fever) in frail elderly patients, so 'well now' does not justify a routine wait.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: 24-hour facility nursing staff available on site
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: On-site nurses enable safe monitored outpatient management (daily skin checks, prompt escalation) once a clinician has examined the limb — but nursing presence does not replace the initial in-person medical assessment.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: Arrange for a doctor to examine her legs in person today (a facility GP/locum visit) to make sure this is not a spreading skin infection before any steroid cream is started. In the meantime, leave the blisters intact — do not scratch or pop them — keep the legs elevated when sitting, and have nursing staff check the legs and her temperature regularly.
baseline_safety_net_advice.contact_method: Facility nursing staff to contact the aged-care GP / after-hours locum service to arrange a same-day in-person review; nurses to remain the point of contact for monitoring.
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: If review cannot happen today through the usual GP, use the after-hours home-visiting or locum service; escalate to the emergency department if fever or systemic features develop before then.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: Provide the reviewing clinician with the onset and evolution of the bullae, the vital signs recorded by nursing staff, the absence of fever/spreading warmth and the clear blister fluid, and note that a skin biopsy with direct immunofluorescence may be needed to confirm the diagnosis.

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Treating the eruption as a benign skin problem for self-care or routine (1–2 week) review, leaving a frail resident with an infective mimic (bullous cellulitis) that telehealth cannot exclude.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: An evolving cellulitis progresses undetected to sepsis in an elderly patient with reduced reserve, with a limb- or life-threatening outcome.

serious_under_triage.failure_description: Booking prompt (24–48 hour) review rather than same-day assessment, allowing an infective mimic to progress a full day in a frail host.

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: Dispatching a 000 ambulance / forcing an immediate ED transfer for a stable, afebrile, systemically-well localised bullous eruption — the unnecessary hospital transfer the source case explicitly avoided, with attendant distress and delirium risk for a frail resident.

clinical_uncertainty_handling.key_uncertainty: Whether this is a non-infective autoimmune blistering process or an early bullous cellulitis — a distinction that hinges on findings (warmth, induration, Nikolsky, bloods) unobtainable via telehealth.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.correct_response_to_uncertainty: State the likely non-infective impression as provisional, explicitly keep cellulitis in play, and resolve the uncertainty with same-day in-person examination plus safety-netting — do not commit to either diagnosis remotely.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.wrong_response_to_uncertainty: Collapse the uncertainty prematurely — either by confidently diagnosing the autoimmune cause and standing down, or by assuming infection and starting empirical antibiotics/hospital transfer.
